title: Stripping a painted pine table
subtitle: Apply stripper, scrape back to bare wood, sand and seal
excerpt: How to strip multiple layers of paint from a pine table using water-based chemical stripper: applying and leaving the stripper, scraping back to bare timber, neutralising, sanding, and applying a sanding sealer coat before finishing.
sourceNotes: Furniture stripping practice. English Heritage guidance on painted pine furniture. HSE guidance on chemical paint strippers. Guild of Master Craftsmen guide to furniture finishing (2001).

recipeTools.notes: For neutralising the stripper residue before finishing.
To protect the floor from stripper drips.
For scoring through thick paint layers before applying stripper.

aliases: how to strip paint from pine furniture

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin shellac or lacquer applied as the first finish coat, which seals the timber and stiffens the surface fibres so they sand back to a clean smooth surface. Built up under a topcoat.

content.text: Pine stripped back to bare wood takes stain and oil finishes well, but only if all paint residue is removed and the grain is clean. Water-based chemical stripper is the standard approach for furniture indoors: it lifts multiple paint layers without raising the grain as aggressively as solvent strippers, and it does not require the ventilation that solvent products demand.
Preparation
Step 1, prepare the area.

Lay a dust sheet under the table. Wear nitrile gloves and safety glasses throughout; water-based stripper is alkaline and irritates skin and eyes on contact. Open windows for ventilation. Remove any hardware from the table before starting.
Step 2, score very thick paint layers.

If the table has more than three layers of paint, score through the paint surface in a crosshatch pattern with the utility knife before applying stripper. This lets the chemical penetrate faster and reduces the number of applications needed. Do not score so deeply that the knife marks the pine.
Applying the stripper
Step 3, apply a generous coat of stripper.

Brush the water-based stripper onto the surface in a thick, even layer. Do not brush it out thinly. The stripper must sit on the surface and soak down through the paint; a thin coat dries before it has penetrated. Apply in one direction; do not go back over a wet area. Cover approximately one square metre at a time.
Step 4, wait for the paint to blister.

Leave the stripper to work according to the manufacturer's instructions, typically 20 to 45 minutes. The paint surface will visibly blister and wrinkle as the stripper works down from the top layer. Do not disturb the surface until the paint has lifted fully.
Scraping and cleaning
Step 5, scrape off the softened paint.

Work the scraper at a shallow angle, pushing the softened paint off the surface into a pile at the edge. Use light pressure; the goal is to lift the paint, not gouge the pine. Drop the lifted paint into a lined bin rather than back onto the work surface. For carved or moulded areas, use a stiff bristle brush or a wooden tool to remove paint from recesses.
Step 6, re-apply for remaining layers.

If paint remains after the first scrape, apply a second coat of stripper to the areas still covered and repeat. Most painted pine tables need two applications. Bare pine should be visible after the second scrape.
Step 7, neutralise with methylated spirit.

Wipe the entire surface with methylated spirit on a clean cloth. This neutralises the alkaline stripper residue that remains in the grain. If left unneutralised, the residue prevents oil and varnish finishes from curing properly. Allow the surface to dry for 2 to 4 hours before sanding.
Sanding and sealing
Step 8, sand the grain clean.

Sand the entire surface along the grain with 120-grit then 180-grit paper on a sanding block. Remove any remaining paint in recesses or edges by hand. Wipe the surface with a clean damp cloth to raise any loose grain fibres, allow to dry, then sand again with 240-grit.
Step 9, apply a sanding sealer.
Apply one coat of
sanding sealer
across the bare pine. This seals the open grain and provides a stable base for whichever topcoat follows. Allow to dry and sand back lightly with 240-grit before applying the finish.
Common mistakes
Finish not curing after stripping.
The stripper was not neutralised with methylated spirit before finishing. Strip the fresh finish back with fine abrasive and wipe with methylated spirit before re-applying.
Grain raised badly after applying the finish.
The sealer step was skipped. Pine has an open grain that needs sealing before topcoats; without the sanding sealer the finish soaks unevenly into early and late wood and produces a blotchy result.
